prompt:
Why is the current case study order on the home page Lacework, the design
system, Goldman Sachs? Goldman Sachs should be at the top. I don't know
starting from which point it was moved up, and this is just wrong. I've
never said it should be ordered this way. Trace when this happened, fix
it, and document the rule so we don't slide back into the wrong order.
response:
## Diagnosis

I don't have access to your repository, git history, or file system, so I **cannot trace the exact commit** where the order changed. I'll be transparent about that rather than fabricate a blame trail.

What I *can* do is give you the canonical fix pattern, the likely root cause class, and a governance rule to prevent recurrence.

---

## Likely Root Cause

**AP-072 – Implicit Ordering by Insertion/Array Index Without an Explicit `order` Field**

Case studies are almost certainly stored as either:
1. An array in a Payload CMS collection/global where the sort order defaults to `createdAt` or document-insertion order, **or**
2. A hardcoded array in a Next.js component/page file where someone added or moved an entry without realizing it controlled display order.

When no explicit, human-readable `order` field governs the sort, any edit to the list (adding a new case study, reseeding the DB, reordering for editorial reasons elsewhere) silently corrupts the intended display order. This is the canonical "implicit ordering" anti-pattern.

---

## Fix

### Step 1 – Find the source of truth

Run this to locate where case studies are defined:

```bash
# Find Payload collection definition
grep -r "caseStud" src/ --include="*.ts" -l

# Find any hardcoded array
grep -r "lacework\|goldman\|design.system" src/ --include="*.tsx" --include="*.ts" -l -i
```

---

### Path A – Hardcoded array in a component or page

The file will look something like `src/app/(frontend)/page.tsx` or `src/components/CaseStudies.tsx`:

```ts
// WRONG – implicit order, fragile
const caseStudies = [
  { slug: "lacework", ... },
  { slug: "design-system", ... },
  { slug: "goldman-sachs", ... },
];
```

**Fix:**

```ts
// RIGHT – explicit order field, self-documenting
const CASE_STUDY_ORDER: Record<string, number> = {
  "goldman-sachs":  1,   // ← intentional: flagship enterprise project, leads
  "lacework":       2,
  "design-system":  3,
};

const caseStudies = [
  { slug: "goldman-sachs",  ... },
  { slug: "lacework",       ... },
  { slug: "design-system",  ... },
].sort((a, b) => CASE_STUDY_ORDER[a.slug] - CASE_STUDY_ORDER[b.slug]);
```

The `.sort()` makes the order *resilient* — even if someone appends to the array, the displayed order is always governed by `CASE_STUDY_ORDER`, not array position.

---

### Path B – Order comes from Payload CMS (collection query)

**1. Add an explicit `order` field to the collection** (`src/collections/CaseStudies.ts`):

```ts
import type { CollectionConfig } from "payload";

export const CaseStudies: CollectionConfig = {
  slug: "case-studies",
  admin: {
    defaultColumns: ["title", "displayOrder", "updatedAt"],
    // Surface the field prominently so editors see it
    useAsTitle: "title",
  },
  fields: [
    {
      name: "title",
      type: "text",
      required: true,
    },
    // ── ADD THIS ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    {
      name: "displayOrder",
      label: "Display Order (lower = appears first)",
      type: "number",
      required: true,
      defaultValue: 99,
      admin: {
        description:
          "Controls homepage sequence. Goldman Sachs = 1, Lacework = 2, Design System = 3.",
      },
    },
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // ...rest of your fields
  ],
};
```

**2. Set the values in Payload admin** (or via a seed script):

| Title | displayOrder |
|---|---|
| Goldman Sachs | 1 |
| Lacework | 2 |
| Design System | 3 |

**3. Update the query in your page/component** to sort by this field:

```ts
// src/app/(frontend)/page.tsx  (or wherever you fetch)
const { docs: caseStudies } = await payload.find({
  collection: "case-studies",
  sort: "displayOrder",   // ascending: 1 → 2 → 3
  limit: 10,
});
```

---

## Documentation – Governance Rule

Create this file and commit it:

```markdown
<!-- docs/content-rules/case-study-order.md -->

# Case Study Display Order — Canonical Rule

## Intended order (homepage, all surfaces)

| Position | Project        | displayOrder field |
|----------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 (top)  | Goldman Sachs  | 1                  |
| 2        | Lacework       | 2                  |
| 3        | Design System  | 3                  |

## Rationale

Goldman Sachs leads because it is the highest-stakes enterprise engagement
and establishes immediate credibility with target hiring audiences.
Lacework follows as the most recent large-scale product work.
The design system closes as a supporting artifact.

## Rules

1. **Never rely on array position or `createdAt` to imply display order.**
   Always use the explicit `displayOrder` field in Payload or the
   `CASE_STUDY_ORDER` constant in code.

2. **Adding a new case study** requires a deliberate `displayOrder` value.
   Default is 99 (end of list) so new items don't accidentally displace
   Goldman Sachs without a conscious choice.

3. **Any change to this order requires updating this document** in the same
   PR. A PR that reorders case studies without updating this file should
   not be merged.

4. **Anti-pattern reference: AP-072** — implicit ordering by insertion/array
   index. The fix is always an explicit, named order field or constant.
```

---

## Principle

> **Ordering is a product decision, not a data-structure side effect.**
> Any list with a business-meaningful sequence must have an explicit,
> human-readable field that encodes that sequence. The moment ordering is
> implicit — in array index, insertion timestamp, or alphabetical slug —
> it will drift without anyone noticing.
